blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XX
THE GRIFFITHS THEOREMby N. KATZ

We prove the theorem of GRIFFITHS [1], which gives a systematic (though non-constructive) way of « finding » algebraic cycles which are \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-cohomologous to zero, but no multiple of which is algebraically equivalent to zero. The proof given here (due to GROTHENDIECK) is the translation into purely algebraic terms of the original, more or less transcendental, proof of GRIFFITHS. In both of them, it is the irreducibility theorem XVIII 6.7 which is a key point (cf. 3.1.6 and 3.2).
1. The formalism of primitive classes
In all that follows, we suppose fixed a prime number \(\ell\). All the schemes considered are assumed to have characteristics \(\ne\ell\).
(1.0.3) \(f,g\) smooth of relative dimensions \(d_1\) and \(d_2\),
Taking into account that \(X\) and \(Y\) are smooth over \(S\), the properness of \(\pi\) gives us a Gysin morphism (SGA 5 IV)
Localizing on \(S\) (in the étale sense), we also obtain a morphism of sheaves on \(S\)

Definition 1.0.8.

Let \((E_r^{p,q},d_r^{p,q})\) and \(({}'E_r^{p,q},{}'d_r^{p,q})\) be two spectral sequences (in a given abelian category), and \((a,b)\in\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z\). The shift of \(({}'E_r^{p,q},{}'d_r^{p,q})\) by \((a,b)\) is the spectral sequence \(({}''E_r^{p,q},{}''d_r^{p,q})\), with

\[ \tag{1.0.9} \left\{ \begin{aligned} {}''E_r^{p,q}&={}'E_r^{p+a,q+b},\\ {}''d_r^{p,q}&={}'d_r^{p+a,q+b}. \end{aligned} \right. \]

1.0.10. A morphism of spectral sequences from \((E_r^{p,q},d_r^{p,q})\) to \(({}''E_r^{p,q},{}''d_r^{p,q})\) is called a morphism of bidegree \((a,b)\) from \((E_r^{p,q},d_r^{p,q})\) to \(({}'E_r^{p,q},{}'d_r^{p,q})\).
The following lemma is a trivial consequence of the theory of the Gysin morphism (SGA 5, IV).

Lemma 1.1.

There exists a canonical morphism \(\pi_*\) of bidegree \((a,-2(d_1-d_2))\) between the Leray spectral sequences

\[ \tag{1.1.0} R^p\alpha_*R^qf_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i)\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}(\alpha f)_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i) \]

and

\[ \tag{1.1.1} R^p\alpha_*R^qg_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i-d_1+d_2)\Longrightarrow R^{p+q}(\alpha g)_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i-d_1+d_2), \]

which on the term \(E_2\) is given by (1.0.7), and on the abutment by (1.0.5).
1.2. Let us now consider the situation
in which \(S,X,f,Y,g\) are as in (1.0.2)–(1.0.4), but now assuming also \(f\) proper.
where \(\mathrm{pr}_{2*}\) is the Gysin homomorphism. By étale localization on \(S\), \(z\) also defines

Lemma 1.3.

The class \(z\) defines a morphism of bidegree \((0,b-2d_1)\) between the Leray spectral sequences

\[ \tag{1.2.7} H^p(S,R^qf_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i)) \]

and

\[ \tag{1.2.8} H^p(S,R^qg_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1)), \]

which on the terms \(E_2\) is given by (1.2.5), and by (1.2.3) on the abutments.
Proof. Taking into account the formula (1.2.4) and the fact that « multiplication by \(z\) » defines a morphism of bidegree \((0,a)\) of Leray spectral sequences

\[ \tag{1.2.9} H^p(S,R^q(\mathrm{pr}_1f)_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(X\times_S Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(i)) \] \[ \tag{1.2.10} \mathrel{\phantom{H^p(S,R^q(\mathrm{pr}_1f)_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i))}} \mathop{\downarrow}\limits^{z} \qquad H^p(S,R^{q+a}(\mathrm{pr}_1f)_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b))\Longrightarrow H^{p+q+a}(X\times_S Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b)), \]
we need only apply 1.1 to the situation

Corollary 1.4.

In the situation (1.2.1)–(1.2.2), the morphism (1.2.3)

\[ \tag{1.4.1} z:H^q(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\longrightarrow H^{q+a-2d_1}(Y,\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1)) \]

transforms primitive classes (XVIII 5.8.1) into primitive classes, and the following diagram is commutative.

\[ \tag{1.4.2} \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{Prim}^q(X/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))&\xrightarrow{\ z\ }& \operatorname{Prim}^{q+a-2d_1}(Y/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1))\\ \big\downarrow u_f&&\big\downarrow u_g\\ H^1(S,R^{q-1}f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i))&\xrightarrow{\ z\ }& H^1(S,R^{q+a-2d_1-1}g_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1)). \end{array} \]

(where \(u_f,u_g\) are the canonical homomorphisms of XVIII 5.8.1).
1.5. Let \(\bar\eta\) be a geometric point of \(S\) (above a point \(\eta\) of \(S\)), and let
be the class induced by \(z\) on the corresponding geometric fiber of

Proposition 1.5.3.

Suppose \(f,g\) proper, \(S\) annexed, and let \(q\in\mathbb Z\). If the class \(z_{\bar\eta}\) induces the zero homomorphism

\[ \tag{1.5.3} 0=z_{\bar\eta}:H^{q-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\longrightarrow H^{q+a-2d_1-1}(Y_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1)), \]

then, for every \(x\in\operatorname{Prim}^q(X/S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\), we have

\[ \tag{1.5.4} u_g(z(x))=0\quad\text{in}\quad H^1(S,R^{q+a-2d_1-1}g_*\mathbb Q_\ell(i+b-d_1)). \]
Proof. By the commutativity of (1.4.2), it suffices to see that \(z\) induces the zero homomorphism

Now \(f\) and \(g\) being proper and smooth, the sheaves \(R^\bullet f_*\mathbb Q_\ell(\bullet)\) and \(R^\bullet g_*\mathbb Q_\ell(\bullet)\) are twisted constant (SGA 4 XVI 2.2), hence equivalent to their values at \(\bar\eta\), viewed as modules under \(\pi_1(S,\bar\eta)\).

(It is a \(\mu(t_1-t_2)\)-analogue of this element that Manin studies in [5]).

1.6.5. Let now \(z\) be an algebraic cycle on \(X\times_S Y\) of co-
dimension \(\geq b\) on each fiber of \(X\times_S Y\)(*), which determines a cohomology class (SGA 5 IV ):
and if the cycles \(z(t_1)\) and \(z(t_2)\) are defined, we have (thanks to the compatibility SGA 5 IV of the cup-product with intersections of cycles):
As a corollary of 1.5.3, we then have:

Proposition 1.7.

Let \(t_1\) and \(t_2\) be two sections of \(f:X\longrightarrow S\), where \(f\) is assumed to have connected fibers, and let \(z\) be an algebraic cycle on \(X\times_S Y\) of codimension \(b\), such that the algebraic cycle

\[ \tag{1.7.1} z(t_1)-z(t_2) \]

is defined. Then its cohomology class is primitive (XVIII 5.8.1). If the algebraic cycle \(z_{\bar\eta}\) on \(X_{\bar\eta}\times Y_{\bar\eta}\) induces the zero homomorphism

\[ \tag{1.7.2} z_{\bar\eta}:H^{2d_1-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(d_1)) \xrightarrow{ 0 }H^{2b-1}(Y_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(b)) \]

then

\[ \tag{1.7.3} u_g\bigl(z(t_1)-z(t_2)\bigr)=0\quad\text{in}\quad H^1(S,R^{2b-1}g_*\mathbb Q_\ell(b)), \]

(where \(u_g\) is the homomorphism of XVIII 5.8.1 appearing in 1.4).

footnotes.html: If \(S\) is smooth over a scheme \(T\) (e.g. \(T=\operatorname{Spec}k\), \(k\) a field), it would suffice to assume \(z\) of codimension \(\geq b\) on each fiber above \(T\).

\[ \tag{2.0.2} T\ \text{proper, smooth and connected over }k\ \text{of dimension }d, \]
We define the filtration by co-level \((N^j)\) of \(H^q(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\)
\[ \tag{2.0.6} N^jH^q(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))= \sum_{(T,z)}\operatorname{images}\bigl(\operatorname{cl}(z): H^{q-2j}(T,\mathbb Q_\ell(i-j))\longrightarrow H^q(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\bigr), \]
the sum being extended over the pairs
\[ \tag{2.0.7} \begin{array}{ll} (T,z)&T/k\ \text{proper smooth and connected of dimension }d,\\ &z\ \text{algebraic cycle on }T\times X\ \text{of codimension }j+d. \end{array} \]
It should be noted that one sometimes speaks of the increasing filtration \(F^j\) « by level », defined by
Replacing \((T,z)\) by \((\mathbb P^1\times T,\{\mathrm{pt}\}\times z)\), one sees that

Proposition 2.1.

Let \(X\) be proper, smooth and geometrically connected over a field \(k\), \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\), \(X_{\bar k}=X\otimes_k\bar k\). Then \(\operatorname{gal}(\bar k/k)\), acting on \(H^q(X_{\bar k},\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\), preserves the filtration by co-level.

Proposition 2.2.

The filtration by level is invariant under extension of algebraically closed fields \(k'/k\).

Proposition 2.3.

Let \(S\) be a reduced and irreducible scheme, with generic point \(\eta\), and

\[ \pi:X\longrightarrow S \]

a proper and smooth morphism, with geometrically connected fibers. If, for given integers \(j\) and \(q\), we have for the geometric generic fiber \(X_{\bar\eta}\)

\[ \tag{2.3.1} H^q(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)=N^jH^q(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell), \]

then there exists a nonempty open set \(U\subset S\) such that at every point \(u\in U\), we have for the geometric fiber \(X_{\bar u}\)

\[ \tag{2.3.2} H^q(X_{\bar u},\mathbb Q_\ell)=N^jH^q(X_{\bar u},\mathbb Q_\ell). \]
3.1. From now on, we fix:

\[ \tag{3.1.0} k,\ \text{an algebraically closed field}, \] \[ \tag{3.1.1} X\subset\mathbb P^r,\quad \text{a }k\text{-scheme, projective, smooth and connected, of even dimension }n, \] \[ \text{which satisfies (LV) (XVIII 5.2.2)}. \]
\[ \tag{3.1.2} D=\{H_t\}_{t\in\mathbb P^1}\ \text{a Lefschetz pencil of hypersurfaces of degree }d, \]

\[ \tag{3.1.4} m:X_{\bar\eta}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X_{k\,\overline{k(\eta)}}\quad\text{the inclusion}, \] \[ \tag{3.1.5} E^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)) =\text{the orthogonal of }m^*H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\ \text{in } H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)). \]
We have seen (XVIII 6.7.2) that the Picard-Lefschetz formula implies

\[ \tag{3.1.6} \begin{gathered} \text{If }n\text{ is even, every subgroup of finite index in}\\ \operatorname{gal}(k(\bar\eta)/k(\eta))\ \text{acts irreducibly on}\\ E^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)). \end{gathered} \]

Proposition 3.2.

Let \(D=\{H_t\}\) be a Lefschetz pencil, and suppose \(n=2m\) even. Then either

\[ \tag{3.2.1} H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i))=m^*H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))+N^{m-1}H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)) \]

or else:

(3.2.2) For every curve \(C\) proper and smooth over \(k(\bar\eta)\), and every algebraic cycle \(z\) on \(C\times X_{\bar\eta}\) of codimension \(m\), the induced homomorphism (1.2.3)

\[ \tag{3.2.3} \operatorname{cl}(z):H^1(C,\mathbb Q_\ell(1+i-m))\longrightarrow H^{2m-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)) \]

has its image in \(m^*H^{2m-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\).
Proof. Suppose that the image of such a homomorphism (3.2.3) is not contained in \(m^*H^{n-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\), or, what amounts to the same thing, that its image in

is not zero. As this image is evidently stable under the operations of a subgroup of finite index in \(\operatorname{gal}(k(\bar\eta)/k(\eta))\), the irreducibility (3.1.6) of \(E^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\) can be restated by saying

\[ \begin{aligned} \text{the image of (3.2.3)}&=\text{the image under }\operatorname{cl}(z)\text{ of }H^1(C,\mathbb Q_\ell(1+i-m))\\ &\subset N^{m-1}H^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell(i)), \end{aligned} \]

\[ \tag{3.3.2} V_{\bar\eta},\quad\text{an algebraic cycle of codimension }n-1\text{ on }X_{\bar\eta}\times(X\times_k k(\bar\eta)) \]

(3.4). This condition \(B\) is satisfied if the operation \(\Lambda\) [4, 1.4.2.1] is algebraic (cf. [4, 2.12 (ii)]. It is conjectured that \(\Lambda\) is always algebraic, hence that the condition \(B\) is always satisfied, and one knows how to verify it in numerous cases [4]. It is satisfied trivially for \(X\) a complete intersection of even dimension since then we know (IX 1.6) that

Theorem 4.1.

Let \(X\) be projective, smooth and connected over \(k\), of even dimension \(n=2m\geq4\). Let \(D\) be a Lefschetz pencil of sections by hypersurfaces of degree \(d\). We assume

(4.1.0) \(X\) satisfies (LV) (XVIII 5.2.2),

(4.1.1) the pencil \(D\) satisfies the hypothesis \(A\) (XVIII 5.3)

(4.1.2) the pencil \(D\) satisfies (3.2.2), i.e. (3.2) the vanishing cohomology \(E^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is not of level \(\leq1\),

(4.1.3) the inclusion \(m^*:X_{\bar\eta}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X\times_k k(\bar\eta)\) satisfies the hypothesis \(B\) (3.3).

Then, if an algebraic cycle \(z\) of codimension \(m\) on \(X\) is such that its restriction (as an algebraic cycle) to \(X_{\bar\eta}\) satisfies

\[\tag{4.1.4}z_{\bar\eta}=z|X_{\bar\eta}\quad\text{is algebraically equivalent to zero}\]

Then (4.1.5) \(z\) is \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-cohomologous to zero, i.e. \(\operatorname{cl}(z)\in H^{2m}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(m))\) is zero.

4.2. A remark on the case where \(X\) is a complete intersection. As already remarked (cf. XVIII 5.2.5, and (3.4.1)), the hypotheses (4.1.0) and (4.1.3) are then automatically satisfied. As for the hypothesis (4.1.1), we have seen (XVIII 6.3.4, 6.4) that it is satisfied for a pencil of hypersurfaces of degree \(d>2m\). On the other hand, we will prove in the next exposé the condition (4.1.2) for a « general » pencil of hypersurfaces of degree \(d>2n\) (XXI 5.2). Hence a « general » pencil of hypersurfaces of degree \(d>2n\) satisfies all the hypotheses of Theorem 4.1.

4.3. Returning to the case where \(X\) is arbitrary, it seems very plausible that for a sufficiently large degree \(d\), all the conditions of 4.1 are satisfied for \(D\), at least for \(D\) sufficiently general. We have already seen that this is the case for (4.1.1) (XVIII 6.4), and we have pointed out that it is conjectured that (4.1.0) and (4.1.3) are satisfied in all cases (and it is true in all cases for (4.1.0) if \(\operatorname{car}k=0\)). As for the condition (4.1.2), it is satisfied (thanks to Hodge theory) for \(d\) large if \(\operatorname{car}k=0\), and heuristic arguments (which the reader will find in the appendix to the following exposé) make it plausible that the same still holds if \(\operatorname{car}k>0\). More generally, these arguments (Hodge theory, resp. crystalline cohomology) prove (resp. make plausible) that for a smooth projective variety \(X\) of dimension \(n\), and for every Lefschetz pencil \(D\) of hypersurfaces of sufficiently large degree \(d\) (resp. every « sufficiently general » pencil \(D\) of hypersurfaces of degree \(d\)) the vanishing cohomology \(E^{n-1}(X_{\bar\eta},\mathbb Q_\ell)\) is of level equal to \(n-1\) (i.e. is not of level \(\leq n-3\), i.e. is not of coniveau 1).

\[ \begin{CD} \operatorname{Prim}^{2m}(C/U,\mathbb Q_\ell(1)) @>{Q}>> \operatorname{Prim}^{2m}(X\times U/U,\mathbb Q_\ell(m)) @>{M^*}>> \operatorname{Prim}^{2m}(\widetilde X_U/U,\mathbb Q_\ell(m))\\ @V{u_{\operatorname{pr}_2}}VV @. @VV{u_\rho}V\\ H^1(U,H^{2m-1}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(m))) @>{M^*}>> H^1(U,R^{2m-1}\rho_{U*}\mathbb Q_\ell(m)), \end{CD} \]

5.0. Let \(S\) be proper and smooth over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Denote by
(5.0.1) \(Z^i(S)\) the group of algebraic cycles of codimension \(i\) on \(S\),
(5.0.2) \(Z^i_{\mathrm{alg}}(S)\) the subgroup of \(Z^i(S)\) formed by the cycles algebraically equivalent to zero,
(5.0.3) \(Z^i_{\ell\text{-}\mathrm{coh}}(S)\) the subgroup of \(Z^i(S)\) formed by the cycles whose cohomology class in \(H^{2i}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\) is zero.
(It is conjectured [4] that this group is independent of \(\ell\), which is true at least if \(\operatorname{car}k=0\).) We set
If moreover \(S\) is projective, and equipped with a projective immersion, we set
(5.0.5) \(Z^{i,\mathrm{prim}}(S)\) = the subgroup of \(Z^i(S)\) formed by the cycles which are primitive (i.e. whose cohomology class is),

\[\tag{5.0.6}\operatorname{Prim}^{2i}_{\mathrm{alg}}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))=\text{the sub-}\mathbb Q\text{-vector space of }H^{2i}(S,\mathbb Q_\ell(i))\text{ generated by cohomology classes of primitive algebraic cycles}.\]

Corollary 5.1.

Under the hypotheses of 4.1, we have

\[\tag{5.1.1}\dim_{\mathbb Q}(\operatorname{Grif}^n(X_{\bar\eta})\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q)\geq \dim_{\mathbb Q_\ell}(\operatorname{Prim}^{2n}_{\mathrm{alg}}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))).\]
Indeed, by 4.1.5, the kernel of the composite arrow

is contained in \(Z^n_{\ell\text{-}\mathrm{coh}}(X)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\), so that \(\operatorname{Prim}^{2n}_{\mathrm{alg}}(X,\mathbb Q_\ell(n))\) is a quotient of a subgroup of \(\operatorname{Grif}^n(X_{\bar\eta})\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\), which completes the proof.
Remark 5.2. For \(X\) a quadric hypersurface in \(\mathbb P^{2m+1}\) we have

In particular (4.2), for \(X\) a quadric in \(\mathbb P^5\), the section of \(X\) by a generic hypersurface of degree \(>5\) contains a curve, \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-cohomologous to zero, no multiple of which is algebraically equivalent to zero.
[1] Griffiths, P. On the Periods of Certain Rational Integrals III, to appear in Annals of Mathematics.
[2] Grothendieck, A. Le Groupe de Brauer III (esp §§ 9, 10) in Dix Exposés sur la Cohomologie des Schémas, North Holland, 1968.
[4] Kleiman, S. Algebraic Cycles and the Weil Conjectures, in Dix Exposés sur la Cohomologie des Schémas, North Holland, 1968.
[5] Manin, J. Rational Points of Algebraic Curves over Function Fields, Translations Amer. Math. Soc.
